findings.why_it_matters: On the default state the eye lands on Discover's solid Refresh button, not the actual conversion target. A ghost CTA reads as 'optional/cancel' next to two solid buttons in the same viewport. Conversion-event (a started download) is the entire purpose of this panel — it must be the strongest visual anchor.
findings.recommendation: Promote to solid brand-green primary (same treatment as Discover Refresh). Disable-state stays gray. Mirror at confirm-modal level (see finding #3).
findings.impact: Large lift on first-time and casual users. Manual-panel downloads should jump materially once the CTA stops competing with secondary controls.

findings.title: FUD-heavy legal disclaimer sits directly under the CTA with no trust-balancing copy
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3184-3186 — 'Downloading copyrighted audio may violate YouTube's Terms of Service and copyright law…'; screenshot dl_02_section_default.png shows this is the last thing the user reads before clicking.
findings.why_it_matters: Loss-aversion research is consistent: a legal warning placed adjacent to a conversion button reduces click-through. Phrasing implies the *tool itself* is risky ('may violate') rather than framing the user's own license to the content. There is zero counter-balance (no 'your library, your machine, no telemetry').
findings.recommendation: Move the disclaimer behind a 'Legal & licensing' disclosure link, OR rewrite as a neutral factual line: 'Audio is saved locally to your machine. Make sure you have the rights to the content you download.' Add a small trust signal: 'Local-only · no telemetry · no account required'.
findings.impact: Moderate-to-large lift. First-download abandonment is the single biggest drop-off point and this copy actively triggers it.

findings.title: Confirm modal's default focus + primary color is on Cancel — the *non-conversion* path
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3150-3151 — `<button id="disc-v2-dl-confirm-cancel" class="… primary">Cancel</button>` then `Download anyway`; screenshot dl_07_confirm_modal.png confirms Cancel is solid green, 'Download anyway' is ghost.
findings.why_it_matters: I understand the PRD §5.6 rationale (sticky-Shift + accidental-Enter). But once a user has explicitly Shift-clicked, they have already opted in. The button copy 'Download anyway' (note 'anyway' — implies bad idea) plus inverted color hierarchy plus default focus on Cancel will measurably suppress legitimate album downloads. The label currently *discourages* the action.
findings.recommendation: Keep Cancel as default focus for safety, but (a) make BOTH buttons same visual weight (ghost) OR make 'Download' the solid primary, (b) rename 'Download anyway' → 'Download album' (drop the loaded word), (c) add 'Don't ask again for this session' checkbox so power users who Shift-click 3 albums in a row don't friction-out.
findings.impact: Moderate lift on per-card album conversions; near-zero risk increase because Shift+click is already an intentional gesture.

findings.title: No post-success affordances — 'Saved to…' is a dead text line, not actionable
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:7048 — `statusEl.textContent = '✓ Saved to ' + (d.path …)`; screenshot dl_04_success.png shows a plain text success line with no buttons.
findings.why_it_matters: Conversion funnels do not end at the click — they end at file-in-hand confirmation. Today the user has to (1) trust the text path, (2) open Finder, (3) navigate manually. No 'Reveal in Finder', no 'Play preview', no 'Add to library', no 'Download another' nudge, no 'Copy path'. Each missing affordance is a chance for the user to lose confidence or fail to verify, and they won't return to the panel as often.
findings.recommendation: On success render an inline success card: filename + duration + size, plus buttons: [Reveal in Finder] [Play preview] [Download another] [Copy path]. 'Reveal in Finder' uses a backend POST /api/reveal endpoint (`subprocess.run(['open','-R',path])` on macOS). Keep the success card visible until user dismisses or starts a new download — do NOT auto-hide.
findings.impact: Large lift on repeat-download rate (the most valuable conversion metric — one user downloading 20 albums beats 20 users downloading 1).

findings.title: Error state is a raw stack-trace string with no recovery path
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_05_error.png — '✗ ERROR: ffmpeg returned exit code 1: Conversion failed!'; docs/index.html:7055 surfaces `err.message` verbatim; autocue/download.py leaks raw subprocess output to the SSE stream.
findings.why_it_matters: Error copy is the highest-stakes conversion surface — it determines whether a user retries or abandons forever. Today: (1) ffmpeg is a brand the user does not know and cannot fix, (2) 'exit code 1' is unactionable, (3) no Retry button, (4) no 'Try a different source' affordance, (5) no link to docs or 'Copy error report'. A user who hits this will not click Download again today.
findings.recommendation: Map known errors to human messages: yt-dlp 403/410 → 'YouTube blocked this video — try a different result'; ffmpeg failure → 'Audio conversion failed — the source file may be corrupted. [Retry] [Try a different source]'; network → 'Connection lost. [Retry]'. Always render a [Retry] button and a [Report issue] link. Keep raw error behind 'Show details'.
findings.impact: Large lift on retry rate; this is the single biggest conversion-killer in the current funnel.

findings.title: CTA copy 'Download' is ambiguous — competes with 3 unrelated 'Download' buttons on the same page
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3173 '#dl-go-btn Download'; line 3234 '#download-btn Download XML'; line 2139 '#backup-btn 💾 Download backup XML'; line 2164 'Download backup first →'. All visible/reachable on the same screen.
findings.why_it_matters: Users skim — when 'Download' means four different things, the user pauses to figure out which one is which. This pause is where they sometimes click the wrong one (XML export) and walk away confused or, worse, file a 'broken' bug.
findings.recommendation: Rename: panel CTA → 'Download audio' or 'Get track'. Sticky-bar 'Download XML' → 'Export to Rekordbox'. '⬇ Album' per-card → 'Get album'. Reserve the unqualified verb 'Download' for at most one button on screen.
findings.impact: Moderate lift via reduced misclick + faster scan-and-act.

findings.title: Loading state doesn't tell the user what's happening or how long it will take
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_03_loading.png shows just '38%' and 'Downloading…'; docs/index.html:7051 — `statusEl.textContent = d.percent + '%'` overwrites any descriptive status text.
findings.why_it_matters: A bare percent number with no track name, no phase ('fetching → converting → writing tags'), no ETA, leaves the user unsure if it's progressing or stuck. Worse — once `d.percent` arrives, it overwrites the human-readable `d.status` line, so the user sees less context than before progress started. Drop-offs spike when users abandon a 'stuck-looking' download and never come back.
findings.recommendation: Show: track name being downloaded (echo the resolved title from yt-dlp), current phase ('Fetching audio… / Converting to WAV… / Tagging…'), percent, and ETA (mm:ss). Keep ALL of these visible simultaneously — don't replace the phase line with raw percent.
findings.impact: Moderate lift on completion rate (fewer mid-download abandons / tab-closes).

findings.title: Per-card 'Downloading…' label truncates inside an 80px button — user can't see status
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_06_per_card.png shows '⬇ Downloading...' clipped/wrapped in a small green pill; docs/index.html:7014 `btn.textContent = '⬇ Album'` then 6979 `btn.textContent = 'Downloading…'`. 90px progress bar (line 6946) is too narrow to be a confidence signal.
findings.why_it_matters: Per-card downloads are the *primary* conversion path (the manual panel is the fallback). Yet the in-progress state is visually weaker than the manual panel. Users can't tell if 'Downloading…' is real progress or hung. 90px progress bar reads as decoration, not status.
findings.recommendation: Replace per-card inline progress with an expanded row state that shows: thumbnail dim + 'Downloading X of Y · 38% · 1:23 remaining' inline at the card's full width, with a [Cancel] button. Keep the [⬇ Album] button replaced with [Cancel] for the duration. Identical pattern to the manual-panel for consistency.
findings.impact: Moderate-to-large lift on Discover-driven downloads (highest-intent users).

findings.title: No file-output preview before commit — user can't see what they're going to get
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_08_yt_modal.png — candidates show only title + duration + uploader + view count; docs/index.html:3263-3289. No bitrate, no codec, no file-size estimate, no 'best vs all formats' chooser.
findings.why_it_matters: DJs care about audio quality. Today the user picks a YouTube candidate blind to the resulting audio quality. After download they may discover it's a 96kbps mono upload from 2009 and they wasted 4 minutes. This kills trust in the feature.
findings.recommendation: Per-candidate show: best-available audio bitrate (yt-dlp exposes this in info_dict.formats), estimated file size, codec. Add a global quality dropdown in the modal: 'Best available (default) / 320 kbps / Original'. Pre-flight the file size so users know what they're committing to.
findings.impact: Moderate lift on YT-modal conversion + higher post-download satisfaction.

findings.title: Inline status text is the *only* indicator the file landed — no toast persistence, no badge
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:7048 `showToast('Download complete')` is the only persistent signal; toast auto-dismisses in ~3s. Per-card success: line 7000 `statusEl.textContent = '✓ saved'` — 6-char string, no path.
findings.why_it_matters: If the user tabbed away during a download (very common — downloads take 30s-3min), they come back to NO indication a download succeeded except the small text line. Conversion isn't just clicking the button — it's the user *knowing* they got the file. Right now the success signal is too quiet to register.
findings.recommendation: Add a persistent 'Recent downloads' list to the panel (last 5 sessions): filename, timestamp, [Reveal] [Play]. Survives until manual clear. Also fire a system Notification API ping (with permission) when downloads finish in a background tab.
findings.impact: Large lift on perceived reliability + repeat-download rate.

findings.title: Destination path is a non-clickable code block — user can't verify, change, or open the folder
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3176-3178 — `` is text-only; 'Switch to AutoCue folder' is a tiny underline link easy to miss; no 'Open folder' button.
findings.why_it_matters: Trust-signal failure: power users want to verify destination *before* clicking. The 'Switch' link only toggles between two preset paths — there's no folder picker. Many DJs have a specific 'incoming' folder for new music that isn't either default.
findings.recommendation: Render destination as: [folder icon] /Users/…/source [Change…] [Open folder]. 'Change…' opens a backend-driven folder picker (or input + remembered list). 'Open folder' reveals current path. Click the path itself to copy.
findings.impact: Small-to-moderate lift via increased pre-click confidence.

findings.title: Mobile layout buries the CTA below 600px of unrelated Discover content
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_09_mobile.png at 390x844 — Download section is below the fold, requires scroll past Discover panel + suggestion cards + watchlist. CTA itself appears at ~70% scroll depth.
findings.why_it_matters: Mobile-first DJs (more common than you'd think — phone for crate research, laptop for sets) hit Discover tab and never see the Download panel. Discoverability of the conversion surface = 0 on first session.
findings.recommendation: On mobile, sticky a compact 'Download' button to the bottom action bar (next to the existing player controls) that scrolls to or opens the Download panel as a sheet. Alternatively, move the Download panel above the suggestion list on mobile widths only.
findings.impact: Moderate lift on mobile conversion (currently near-zero).

findings.title: Help text leaks implementation detail and self-references — kills portability and confidence
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3160 'Fetch audio from YouTube with yt-dlp. Use the ⬇ Album button on any suggestion above…'; line 3147 confirm modal 'This will fetch audio from YouTube via yt-dlp.'
findings.why_it_matters: (1) 'yt-dlp' is a developer term — non-technical users see jargon and lose confidence the product is for them. (2) 'on any suggestion above' breaks the moment this component is reused elsewhere (PRD goal). (3) References to internal architecture signal 'this is a hobby tool', not a polished product.
findings.recommendation: Help text: 'Get audio from a YouTube link or search. Saved as WAV to your music folder.' Confirm modal body: 'Save "{album_name}" to {folder}?' (use actual values). Drop every yt-dlp / ffmpeg mention from user-facing strings — keep them in error-details and the install banner only.
findings.impact: Small-to-moderate lift via perceived professionalism + portability.

findings.title: Three near-identical download SSE drivers — no shared module
findings.evidence: docs/index.html runDownload() lines 6974-7016, downloadManual() lines 7018-7061, _ytDownload() lines 10807-10858. Each re-implements fetch('/api/download'), SSE consumption, progress mapping, error toast, finally-cleanup. _ytDownload even bypasses the shared _consumeSSE helper and re-parses 'data: ' frames inline.
findings.why_it_matters: Three sources of truth for the same state machine. A bug fix (better error formatting, AbortController, cancel button, format selector) has to be made in 3 places and history shows that doesn't happen — _ytDownload has its own AbortController while runDownload/downloadManual have none. PRD adds format dropdown, normalize toggle, ID3 toggle — each one will fork further if not consolidated first.
findings.recommendation: Extract a single `_Download` IIFE that owns one state machine: `_Download.start({query, opts, ui})` where `ui` is a render-callback receiving `{state:'idle|loading|success|error', percent, message, path, error}`. All 6 surfaces become thin views. Internally hold one AbortController, one current job id, expose `_Download.cancel(jobId)`. Mirrors the pattern already proven by `_setBtnCancellable` + enrichComments() at line 7080.
findings.impact: Removes ~150 lines of duplication, makes new PRD features one-place changes, eliminates divergent error UX between surfaces.

findings.title: Raw backend exception text leaks into status UI
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_05_error.png shows: `× ERROR: ffmpeg returned exit code 1: Conversion failed!`. Source: downloadManual() line 7047 (`statusEl.textContent = '✗ ' + (d.error || 'failed')`) and runDownload() line 7011. Backend forwards `str(exc)` verbatim — routes.py line 2732 `result['error'] = str(exc)` emitted at line 2754.
findings.why_it_matters: Users see unparsed yt-dlp/ffmpeg internals (`exit code 1`, `403 Forbidden`, `Sign in to confirm you're not a bot`, `ERROR: Unsupported URL`). They can't act on this and lose confidence. PRD explicitly calls out an Error state — it must be classified, not pasted.
findings.recommendation: Add `_Download._classifyError(rawMsg)` mapper returning `{kind, userMessage, hint, technicalDetails}`. Map: /403|sign in|bot/i → 'YouTube blocked this video. Try a different URL.'; /ffmpeg|exit code/i → 'Audio conversion failed — check that ffmpeg is up to date.'; /unsupported url|no video/i → 'Not a YouTube URL or supported source.'; default → 'Download failed.' Render userMessage in status, hide technicalDetails behind a `Show details` toggle.
findings.impact: Users get actionable copy in the same pixel space; raw stack-trace strings stay accessible for bug reports but don't dominate the panel.

findings.title: No AbortController on runDownload/downloadManual — no cancel possible
findings.evidence: runDownload() lines 6974-7016 and downloadManual() lines 7018-7061: no `signal:` passed to fetch. Compare _ytDownload() lines 10809-10824 which does use `_ytDownloadAbort = new AbortController()`. Compare enrichComments line 7080 — same pattern that should be standard.
findings.why_it_matters: A user who pastes the wrong URL or kicks off a 1-hour mix has no way out except closing the tab; the backend keeps yt-dlp running until completion. Server already supports cancel (routes.py line 2748 sets `cancel_event` on client disconnect, download.py line 137 raises `DownloadCancelled`) — the frontend just never disconnects.
findings.recommendation: In the unified `_Download.start`, always create an `AbortController`, pass `signal:` to fetch, and during loading state swap the primary button to `Cancel` (reuse `_setBtnCancellable` from line 7080-7087). On Abort, render an `idle` state with toast 'Download cancelled'.
findings.impact: Users regain control; backend resource leaks (long yt-dlp processes after tab nav) stop. Required for the new batch flow.

findings.title: setTimeout(1000) clears success state before the user reads it
findings.evidence: downloadManual() line 7059: `setTimeout(() => { progress.style.display = 'none'; }, 1000);`. The progress bar is hidden but `statusEl.textContent = '✓ Saved to /path/…'` (line 7048) remains — and a second download will overwrite it instantly. No explicit Success state lifecycle.
findings.why_it_matters: PRD requires explicit Idle/Loading/Success/Error states. Today the bar disappears in 1s while the success text lingers ambiguously; on the next attempt the prior outcome is silently overwritten. Users cannot tell whether `✓ Saved to /…` is from this attempt or the previous one.
findings.recommendation: Replace setTimeout-driven cleanup with an explicit state machine: `idle → loading → success | error`. Success persists with a 'Saved to <folder> — Open in Finder | Download another' affordance until the user starts a new job or clicks dismiss. No automatic clear.
findings.impact: Confidence rises; the success path becomes the obvious place to chain the next action (open folder, copy path, download another).

findings.title: Two concurrent downloads have no mutual-exclusion guard
findings.evidence: Per-card runDownload (line 6974) and manual downloadManual (line 7018) both POST `/api/download` and can be invoked back-to-back. There is no shared in-flight flag. Click ⬇ Album on a card while the manual panel is mid-download → two parallel SSE streams write into different DOM nodes but both yt-dlp processes hammer the same `dest_dir`.
findings.why_it_matters: Two yt-dlp workers writing into the same folder can collide on `outtmpl '%(title)s.%(ext)s'` (download.py line 151) if searches resolve to the same title, and they share an ffmpeg saturation budget. The user thinks they queued, but the UI shows only one bar — the other is orphaned.
findings.recommendation: In `_Download`, hold one global `currentJob` ref. If a second start arrives, either (a) reject with toast 'A download is already in progress — Cancel it or wait', or (b) move to an explicit queue with 'Download X of Y' UI (also what PRD requires for albums). Reuse the album backend's processed/total events for both single and batch paths.
findings.impact: Eliminates a real race; provides a free upgrade path to the PRD's batch progress UI.

findings.title: No debounce / Enter handler / URL validation on #dl-query
findings.evidence: docs/index.html line 3170 defines `#dl-query` input and line 4586 wires only the button click — `dlBtn.addEventListener('click', downloadManual)`. No `keydown` Enter handler (compare yt-query line 10803 which has one). No client-side URL sniff before POST.
findings.why_it_matters: Enter is the standard submit gesture on a single-input form; not wiring it is a paper cut every keyboard user notices. The backend wastes time + bandwidth on obvious garbage (empty paste, `http:/typo.com`, multi-line clipboard contents) that a 50-line client check would reject.
findings.recommendation: Add `dl-query.addEventListener('keydown', e => { if (e.key === 'Enter') downloadManual(); })`. Add a lightweight client validator `_Download._classifyQuery(s)` → `{type:'url'|'search'|'invalid', normalized}` that trims, strips control chars, rejects multi-line input, and shows an inline hint chip `URL detected` or `Will search YouTube` so the user knows what's about to happen.
findings.impact: Better keyboard ergonomics and instant validation feedback; avoids round-trip for trivially wrong input.

findings.title: Inconsistent button copy after completion tied to surface — fragile when refactoring
findings.evidence: runDownload() line 7014 hardcodes `btn.textContent = '⬇ Album'` in the finally. downloadManual() line 7058 hardcodes `btn.textContent = 'Download'`. Each function assumes its caller's button label. Move a button or change copy → silent regression.
findings.why_it_matters: Today's per-card card might tomorrow be reused in a tracklist row, in a modal, or in a different language; resetting the label inside the handler couples view + controller. Also breaks i18n later.
findings.recommendation: Capture the original label at handler entry (`const original = btn.textContent`) and restore that; or — preferable — let the new `_Download` module not touch button text at all and instead emit state events the caller renders. Caller owns labels.
findings.impact: Removes a known regression vector when copy or layout changes.

findings.title: _ytDownload re-implements SSE parsing instead of using _consumeSSE
findings.evidence: _ytDownload lines 10827-10853 manually loops `reader.read()`, decodes, splits on `\n\n`, slices `data: `, and JSON.parses. The shared helper `_consumeSSE` is used by runDownload (line 6993), downloadManual (line 7044), enrichComments (line 7105), and many other surfaces.
findings.why_it_matters: Duplicated SSE parsing means duplicated bugs: _consumeSSE handles partial frames and disconnect; _ytDownload does not. Any future improvement to _consumeSSE (backoff, structured event types, telemetry) skips this surface.
findings.recommendation: Replace the inline loop with `await _consumeSSE(resp, ev => { … }, _ytDownloadAbort.signal)` — same as runDownload. Delete ~25 lines.
findings.impact: Quietly fixes any future parser edge case across all surfaces and reduces engineering surface area.

findings.title: Listener wired before component lifecycle — couples to initial DOM render
findings.evidence: Line 4586: `if (dlBtn) dlBtn.addEventListener('click', downloadManual)` runs at init. Line 4595: `document.getElementById('download-controls').style.display = ready ? '' : 'none'`. Works today because the button exists in DOM regardless of capability — but a future re-render of the panel HTML will silently lose the listener.
findings.why_it_matters: Future-proofing matters once the component is extracted. Today wiring is OK; when `<download-component>` is templated, the direct getElementById wiring will break without errors.
findings.recommendation: Use event delegation: `document.addEventListener('click', e => { if (e.target.closest('#dl-go-btn')) downloadManual(); })`. Mirrors the pattern at line 6963-6971 for `.disc-dl-btn`. Survives any future re-mount.
findings.impact: Robust against panel re-renders (likely once the PRD's component refactor lands).

findings.title: Naming collision — `#download-btn`, `#download-section`, `#download-bar`, `#backup-btn` mix unrelated XML feature with YouTube audio
findings.evidence: Line 3234 `#download-btn` = 'Download XML' (Rekordbox export); line 3193 `#download-bar` = sticky XML import bar; line 2139 `#backup-btn` = 'Download backup XML'; line 3156 `#download-section` = YouTube audio download. Code at line 10347 attaches an XML blob handler to `#download-btn`.
findings.why_it_matters: Code review and grep for 'download' returns mixed results across two completely unrelated features. Higher chance of cross-wiring when refactoring (e.g. accidentally rebinding `#download-btn` to YouTube flow). Already a maintenance hazard, will worsen when the new component lands.
findings.recommendation: Rename the YouTube surface tokens with an explicit prefix while in flight: `#yt-dl-section`, `#yt-dl-go-btn`, `#yt-dl-query`, `#yt-dl-progress`, `#yt-dl-status`, `#yt-dl-dest`. Keep XML IDs untouched. Single search/replace PR before the bigger refactor.
findings.impact: Eliminates an entire class of cross-feature regressions; greps become useful.

findings.title: `_dlDestDir` is free-standing `var` global — mutated from two places, no pub/sub
findings.evidence: Line 4545 `var _dlDestDir = '';`. Mutated at init (line 4593) and in switch handler (line 4619). Read in runDownload (line 6987), downloadManual (lines 7038, 7048), success path (line 7001). No `AppState` pub/sub entry (CLAUDE.md web-ui.md describes that pattern).
findings.why_it_matters: If a download is in-flight and the user clicks 'Switch to AutoCue folder', the in-flight request keeps the original dest_dir on the server, but `_dlDestDir` mutates before the success line `'✓ Saved to ' + _dlDestDir` reads — so the displayed path can disagree with the actual saved path. Subtle bug, no test.
findings.recommendation: Either freeze dest_dir into the in-flight job (capture at start, reference from job state on success) or expose `_Download.subscribe('destChange', cb)` via the new module and have the panel re-render. Always render the path from server-emitted `ev.path` (some branches already do but fall back to `_dlDestDir`).
findings.impact: Eliminates a stale display bug and makes the dest source-of-truth explicit.

findings.title: No structured download event schema — server emits ad-hoc keys per endpoint
findings.evidence: routes.py line 2744 emits `{processed, total, query, status, percent}` for single; line 2788 emits `{processed, total, title, query, status, path, downloaded}` for album; line 2754 emits `{done, status:'error', error, failed}`; line 2756 emits `{done, status:'finished', path, downloaded}`. Frontend handlers check different combinations of these keys in different surfaces.
findings.why_it_matters: Once PRD adds normalize/ID3/format opts, the event surface will sprout more ad-hoc keys (e.g. `phase:'extract'|'normalize'|'tag'`). With no schema or types, drift between server and 6 client call sites is inevitable.
findings.recommendation: Define one event union: `{type:'progress', percent, phase, processed, total, currentTitle} | {type:'done', status:'finished'|'error'|'cancelled', path?, error?, totals:{downloaded, failed, total}}`. Update both endpoints to emit it; update `_Download` to consume only that. Document in autocue/download.py docstring. Lock with a single jsonschema test in pytest.
findings.impact: Stable contract for future features; one place to evolve; trivially testable from Python and JS.

raw.persona: Brand-new user who has never used AutoCue, just clicked into the Discover tab for the first time. I'm a hobbyist DJ. I know what YouTube is. I don't know what "yt-dlp" is, I don't know what an "Album button" is, and I don't know what AutoCue does to the file after it downloads. I'm narrating aloud.

findings.title: First-look narrative: 'What is yt-dlp? I have no idea what this thing does'
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3160 — 'Fetch audio from YouTube with yt-dlp. Use the ⬇ Album button on any suggestion above, or paste a URL / search term below.' Screenshot dl_02_section_default.png.
findings.why_it_matters: Reading aloud: 'Fetch audio from YouTube with yt-dlp.' I stop. 'yt-dlp' — is that a file format? A service? A virus? It feels like a stranger handed me a CLI flag. The very first sentence of the Download panel uses a developer term that no music-listener knows. I lose trust before I even type anything. Then '⬇ Album button on any suggestion above' — there are NO suggestions visible above me (scan failed banner). That sentence is broken in my current context, which makes me think the whole panel is broken.
findings.recommendation: Drop the 'with yt-dlp' clause entirely. Replace with: 'Download a track or album from YouTube as an audio file.' Remove the 'Use the ⬇ Album button on any suggestion above' sentence — it is contextual coupling to a sibling component that may not be present. Component should be self-explanatory standalone.
findings.impact: Restores trust in the first 3 seconds, removes the only piece of jargon, and makes the panel portable.

findings.title: 'Where does the file go?' is whispered in 11px gray instead of being the headline
findings.evidence:
docs/index.html:3175-3178 — `

Saving to ` and `dl-dest-switch` link. Screenshot dl_02_section_default.png shows it as a near-invisible secondary line below the button.
findings.why_it_matters: My #1 question as a new user is 'where will this file go on my computer?' The answer is buried at 11px in the muted color, below the action. I read it AFTER I'd already mentally hit Download — too late if it was wrong. And the path `/Users/henrigeorge/Projects/Music/source` means nothing to me — is that a default? Did AutoCue pick that? Can I change it? The 'Switch to AutoCue folder' link is the same 11px and only appears conditionally, so I miss it entirely.
findings.recommendation: Promote destination to a labelled field with its own line: 'Save to: [folder path] [Choose…]'. Make it 12-13px, not 11px. Always show the 'Choose folder' control, even when the default is already the AutoCue folder. Bonus: show a tiny disk icon and folder name (e.g. 'source/') in larger type with the full path as a tooltip.
findings.impact: Removes a top-3 confidence-killing question. Users will not download to surprise locations.

findings.title: 'What format will I get?' — there is no answer anywhere on the screen
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3166-3183 contains no format selector or format hint. autocue/download.py defaults to `audio_format='mp3', audio_quality='192'`. The success line in dl_04_success.png reveals '.mp3' only AFTER the download.
findings.why_it_matters: I'm a DJ. Format matters. I don't know if I'm getting WAV, FLAC, MP3 320, or a 96kbps brick. The UI tells me nothing until the file is already on disk with an .mp3 extension. If I wanted FLAC for mixing, I just wasted bandwidth and have to redo it from CLI somehow. This is a silent-default trap.
findings.recommendation: Add a visible format selector next to the URL input — dropdown labelled 'Format' with WAV / MP3 320 / Original (M4A/Opus). Persist user choice in localStorage. Show the chosen format inline in the status text ('Downloading as WAV…').
findings.impact: Removes silent surprise, gives DJs the quality they actually want without leaving the UI.

findings.title: Legal disclaimer is well-meaning but reads like a lawsuit warning — scary, not reassuring
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3185 — 'Downloading copyrighted audio may violate YouTube's Terms of Service and copyright law. Only download content you are authorised to (your own uploads, Creative-Commons or licensed material). Lawful use is your responsibility.' Screenshot dl_02_section_default.png.
findings.why_it_matters: Reading aloud: 'may violate copyright law'. I freeze. 'Lawful use is your responsibility'. The tone is defensive-lawyer, not 'we trust you, here's the tool'. As a new user I now wonder: is this app sketchy? Is it going to get me in trouble? I almost close the tab. I understand the legal need, but the wording lands closer to 'beware' than 'play responsibly'.
findings.recommendation: Soften and shorten: 'Only download tracks you own or have rights to (your uploads, Creative Commons, or licensed material). [Learn more]' — link to a longer policy page. Move it to a small info icon next to the button rather than always-visible block text. Keeps the legal cover, drops the scary vibe.
findings.impact: Higher conversion from intent to first download. Less first-impression damage.

findings.title: Idle state is indistinguishable from 'unavailable' — button is ghost-styled like a tertiary action
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3173 — `<button id='dl-go-btn' class='secondary-btn'>Download</button>`. Screenshot dl_02_section_default.png shows it as a thin-bordered ghost button identical to 'Switch to AutoCue folder' style language.
findings.why_it_matters: This is the PRIMARY call to action of an entire panel and it is rendered with the same visual weight as 'Cancel' on the confirm modal. As a new user I scan the page looking for the green AutoCue brand button (I see it on 'Refresh' above). Its absence here makes me think this is an experimental/beta feature. I am less likely to click. Compare to the confirm modal where Cancel is the green primary (screenshot dl_07) — totally inverted intent.
findings.recommendation: Make 'Download' the brand-green primary button. Ghost button reserves for secondary actions like 'Switch folder' or 'Cancel queued download'.
findings.impact: Visual hierarchy matches intent. New users find the action immediately.

findings.title: Confirm modal: Cancel is the bright-green primary, Download is the ghost — green means 'go' to me
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3150-3151 — `<button id='disc-v2-dl-confirm-cancel' class='disc-v2-detail-action primary'>Cancel</button> <button id='disc-v2-dl-confirm-go' class='disc-v2-detail-action'>Download anyway</button>`. Screenshot dl_07_confirm_modal.png clearly shows green Cancel left, ghost 'Download anyway' right.
findings.why_it_matters: I shift-clicked Album by accident (didn't even realize Shift was modifying the click). Modal pops up. My eye lands on the GREEN button and I expect that to be 'continue' — green = go in every traffic light, every UI convention I've ever seen. I click it. It cancels. I lose my action and have to redo. OR worse: as muscle memory, users who DO want to download keep clicking green and keep cancelling, then assume the feature is broken. The phrase 'Download anyway' on the ghost button also reads adversarial — 'anyway' implies I'm doing something wrong.
findings.recommendation: Invert: 'Download album' = green primary; 'Cancel' = ghost/secondary. Drop the word 'anyway'. Set default focus on Cancel (which it already does) but visually elevate Download. Standard pattern: destructive/dangerous = red primary, neutral confirmations = brand primary, dismiss = ghost.
findings.impact: Removes the worst single mis-click trap in the whole download surface set.

findings.title: Error state leaks raw ffmpeg exit codes — meaningless to me, terrifying to a beginner
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_05_error.png: '✗ ERROR: ffmpeg returned exit code 1: Conversion failed!'. autocue/download.py raises raw subprocess errors that propagate through the SSE stream into the UI status.
findings.why_it_matters: I have no idea what ffmpeg is. 'exit code 1' could mean 'no internet', 'wrong URL', 'file already exists', 'YouTube blocked us', or 'your disk is full'. I have no path to recovery. There is no 'Try again' button, no 'See log', nothing. The toast also just says 'Download failed' which I missed in the corner. I will assume the app is broken and stop using the feature.
findings.recommendation: Map known yt-dlp/ffmpeg errors to user language: network errors → 'Could not reach YouTube. Check your connection.'; 403/age-gated → 'This video requires sign-in or is unavailable.'; disk full → 'Not enough space in [folder].'. Always show a 'Try again' affordance and a 'Copy error details' link for support. Never display 'exit code N' to end users.
findings.impact: Turns dead-end failures into recoverable ones. Cuts support burden.

findings.title: Per-card ⬇ Album button: no idea what 'Album' means — single track? Whole album? Both?
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:6920-7016 per-card download. Screenshot dl_06_per_card.png shows '⬇ Album' on a Selected Ambient Works II card (which is an LP) — but the label is the same word AutoCue uses for the metadata field.
findings.why_it_matters: I'm looking at a card for 'Selected Ambient Works II' tagged LP. The button says '⬇ Album'. Does this download the whole album? Or just this one card representing the album? The backend has `noplaylist: True` so it's actually NOT downloading the album — just one track. The label lies. As a new user I'd expect to get all 12 tracks. When I get one file I'm confused and disappointed.
findings.recommendation: Be honest about scope. Either: (a) label the button '⬇ Download' (singular) and let the modal explain what's being fetched; or (b) actually implement playlist/album download (remove `noplaylist: True`) and show 'Downloading 1 of 12…' progress. The PRD explicitly asks for the batch case — implement it.
findings.impact: Removes a class of trust-destroying surprises ('I asked for an album, got one track').

findings.title: Per-card 90px mini progress bar is too thin to read and has no track-name context
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_06_per_card.png — 'Loveless' card shows 'Downloading…' text and '55%' below a 90px-wide hair-thin progress bar inside the right-edge action area.
findings.why_it_matters: I have to squint. The percent is in micro-type. There is no 'Downloading: my bloody valentine — Loveless' label — I have to glance back at the card to remember what I clicked. If I scroll the list, my downloading card scrolls out of view and I lose all progress visibility. The manual panel below has its own progress bar in another place, so progress for the same feature is fragmented across two areas.
findings.recommendation: Use the same canonical Download panel below to show ALL in-flight downloads with track names. Per-card button just flips to 'Downloading…' state with a small spinner. Eliminates the duplicated mini progress UI and gives one place to look.
findings.impact: Consolidates progress visibility. Reduces visual noise on cards.

findings.title: Naming collision: 'Download XML' in sticky bar reads as 'Download' to a new user
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3234 `<button class='primary' id='download-btn'>Download XML</button>` in #download-bar; docs/index.html:2139 #backup-btn '💾 Download backup XML'; docs/index.html:2164 'Download backup first →' link.
findings.why_it_matters: There are now FOUR things on screen with the word 'Download': the panel labelled 'Download', the per-card '⬇ Album' (downloads), the sticky-bar 'Download XML' (totally different — exports Rekordbox XML), and 'Download backup XML' in another corner. As a brand-new user my eye is drawn to the most prominent GREEN button which is 'Download XML' in the sticky bar. I click it expecting to download a track. I get an XML file. I have no idea what to do with that. The naming collision actively misleads.
findings.recommendation: Rename the sticky-bar button to 'Export Rekordbox XML' or 'Apply / Export'. Rename '💾 Download backup XML' to 'Save backup XML'. Reserve the word 'Download' (in user-facing labels) for the audio-fetch feature, since that's how 99% of users think about that word.
findings.impact: Disambiguates the entire app's primary verb. Eliminates a mis-click trap that wastes a backup-XML download.

findings.title: Mobile layout shoves the Download panel below a dead 'Refresh' card — feature is hidden
findings.evidence: Screenshot dl_09_mobile.png 390x844: I have to scroll past the failed Discover scan area, then past mock cards, then I finally see Download. The folder switcher link wraps to a new line under '/Users/.../source' which I can barely read.
findings.why_it_matters: On a 390px phone screen, the Download panel is below-the-fold by 2 swipes. The path `/Users/henrigeorge/Projects/Music/source` truncates oddly. The 'Switch to AutoCue folder' link wraps. The input is full-width but the button is to the right at full-text length, leaving an awkward two-line layout. I'd assume the app isn't designed for mobile and either bounce or pretend it's desktop-only.
findings.recommendation: On <600px: stack input above button (button full-width). Truncate path with middle-ellipsis and full path on tap. Surface a Download CTA earlier — maybe a floating 'Download a track' button. Consider hiding the entire Discover panel by default on mobile if scans don't work well there.
findings.impact: Makes feature usable on phones. Today it is effectively desktop-only by accident.

findings.title: No feedback that download started — button text changes to 'Downloading…' but progress bar jumps to 38% instantly with no track name
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:7026 `fill.style.width = '5%'` initial, then jumps to whatever yt-dlp percent reports. Screenshot dl_03_loading.png shows '38%' with no current-stage label.
findings.why_it_matters: I clicked Download. The bar is at 38%. 38% of what? Downloading? Converting? Postprocessing? It just says '38%'. The track I asked for ('Aphex Twin - Avril 14th') doesn't appear anywhere in the status — only in the input box that I'm now mentally disconnected from. I have no way to know if I should wait 5 seconds or 5 minutes.
findings.recommendation: Status template: 'Downloading [Aphex Twin – Avril 14th] · 38% · ~12s left'. Always include the title and ETA (yt-dlp provides it). After download, show 'Converting to MP3…' while ffmpeg runs (separate sub-stage). Phase the progress bar in two colors or with sub-labels.
findings.impact: Users know what's happening at every moment. Reduces 'is it stuck?' aborts.

findings.title: Success state has no 'Play' or 'Reveal in Finder' action — file vanishes into the folder
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:7048 — on success the status shows '✓ Saved to /Users/.../Avril 14th.mp3' and that's it. Screenshot dl_04_success.png.
findings.why_it_matters: I just downloaded a file. I want to verify it (play it), or open the folder to drag it into Rekordbox. Neither is offered. I have to manually open Finder, navigate to the path, find the file. As a new user I now have to learn 'how do I get from this success message to actually using the file' — a friction point that feels like the workflow ended mid-sentence.
findings.recommendation: Success state shows three actions: 'Play preview' (in-app), 'Reveal in Finder' (opens enclosing folder via a backend hook or copies path), 'Download another'. Persist the most-recent download path in a small history list (last 3).
findings.impact: Closes the loop. User actually uses what they downloaded.

findings.title: Input has no aria-label, no autofocus, no Enter-to-submit feedback announcement
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3170-3171 — `<input type='text' id='dl-query' placeholder='e.g. https://youtu.be/… or "Artist – Title"'>`. No aria-label, no aria-describedby pointing to the legal disclaimer or destination, no live region for the status `#dl-status`.
findings.why_it_matters: Screen reader users hear 'edit, blank' — they have no idea what this input is for unless they navigate up to the visual label. The status text changes during download but is not in an aria-live region, so SR users get no progress announcements. The button doesn't expose a busy state via aria-busy. Pressing Enter in the input does not submit (no keydown handler), so I have to mouse to the Download button every time.
findings.recommendation: Add aria-labelledby pointing to the visible label, aria-describedby pointing to the destination/legal text. Wrap #dl-status and #dl-progress in role='status' aria-live='polite'. Add aria-busy on the panel during download. Wire Enter on #dl-query → click #dl-go-btn.
findings.impact: Makes the feature usable for screen readers and keyboard-only DJs (not rare in this community).

findings.title: Confirm modal body ('This will fetch audio from YouTube via yt-dlp.') is the same jargon problem in a higher-stakes context
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3146-3148. Screenshot dl_07_confirm_modal.png.
findings.why_it_matters: I shift-clicked. A modal blocks the page. The body explains the action using the exact term ('yt-dlp') I don't understand. The modal is a teachable moment — instead the copy adds friction. There is also no information about WHAT I'm about to download (which album?), so I can't even verify intent before confirming.
findings.recommendation: Body should show the actual album/track: 'Download Loveless – my bloody valentine from YouTube as audio?'. Drop the 'via yt-dlp' phrase entirely.
findings.impact: User can confirm by recognising the title, not by guessing what they triggered.

findings.title: YouTube candidate modal: ambiguous 'Pick' verb and only one progress bar shared across all candidates
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3263-3289. Screenshot dl_08_yt_modal.png shows two candidates each with 'Pick' button and a single progress bar/'Downloading… 25%' below the second candidate.
findings.why_it_matters: 'Pick' is unusual — 'Download', 'Use this', or 'Select' would be clearer. While download is in flight, the other 'Pick' button is still enabled-looking — can I queue a second one? What happens if I click? The shared progress bar floats under the candidates with no clear visual binding to which one is in progress. I can't see what's playing or even visually confirm 'this is the 1986 version, not the 2017 reissue'.
findings.recommendation: Rename 'Pick' to 'Download this version'. Disable other candidate buttons while one is in flight. Move the progress bar INTO the row of the candidate being downloaded (inline), with a clear ✕ to cancel.
findings.impact: Confidence that the right version is being grabbed. No accidental double-downloads.

raw.persona: DJ power-user with 5,000+ tracks, downloads daily in batches from playlists. Lives in the keyboard, hates clicking, retries failures often.

findings.title: No Enter-to-submit on URL field — paste-and-go workflow is broken
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3170-3173 (<input id="dl-query"> has no keydown handler) and 7018-7061 (downloadManual is wired only to btn click, no form/keydown listener registered).
findings.why_it_matters: The single most common power-user flow is Cmd+V then Enter. I have to paste a URL then mouse over to the Download button 50+ times a day. Every other URL input on the web (browser omnibox, Spotify search, YouTube search) submits on Enter. Forcing mouse travel here is a 1-2 second tax per download that compounds enormously over a typical 30-track playlist session.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the input + button in a <form> and listen for submit (preventDefault then downloadManual()), OR add input.addEventListener('keydown', e => { if (e.key === 'Enter') downloadManual(); }). Also surface the affordance: change the placeholder to 'Paste URL or type "Artist – Title" — Enter to download'.
findings.impact: Cuts ~1.5s + one full mouse-move per download. Over a 30-track batch that's roughly a minute of friction and several missed-click context switches.

findings.title: No global keyboard shortcut to focus the Download input
findings.evidence: Grep of docs/index.html shows no keybinding pointing at 'dl-query'. The Discover tab has shortcuts (per docs/reference/discover-v2.md) but none route to Download. The download section is below-the-fold (see dl_02_section_default.png — it scrolls off on a normal viewport).
findings.why_it_matters: I'm typically working in Library or Cues, find a hole, want to grab the track. Today I have to: switch to Discover tab → scroll to the bottom → click the input → paste. A '/' or 'd' shortcut from anywhere ('focus download') would collapse that to one keystroke. Power users live on shortcuts; the rest of the app teaches that habit (refresh, etc.) and then this surface drops it.
findings.recommendation: Register a global keybinding (e.g. Shift+D or Cmd+Shift+Y) that switches to Discover tab, scrolls #download-section into view, and focuses #dl-query. Show it in the placeholder and in the keyboard-shortcut help dialog.
findings.impact: Reduces a 3-action cross-tab workflow to a single keystroke. For 30 downloads/day that's ~90 fewer clicks.

findings.title: yt-dlp is hardcoded to noplaylist=True — pasting a YouTube playlist URL silently downloads only the first track
findings.evidence: autocue/download.py:154 'noplaylist': True. The manual input accepts any URL but downloadManual() (docs/index.html:7035) routes everything through /api/download which uses this fixed option. There is no batch/playlist code path on the manual surface.
findings.why_it_matters: This is THE flagship power-user use case: 'I found a great Boiler Room set on YouTube, grab the tracklist'. Today I paste the playlist URL, the system silently grabs track 1 and reports '✓ Saved'. There's no warning, no 'this is a playlist of 24 tracks, download all?' prompt. The PRD calls out playlist/album batch but the manual panel today actively misleads users into thinking they got what they asked for.
findings.recommendation: Detect playlist URLs client-side (list=, /playlist?) and either (a) auto-flip to a batch flow with 'Downloading track X of Y' or (b) prompt 'This URL is a playlist with N tracks — download all, or just the first video?' Default to all. Backend: drop noplaylist or thread it through a request option.
findings.impact: Unlocks batch download of any YouTube playlist or album. For a DJ this is 10-30 tracks per paste vs. 1 today.

findings.title: No recent-history / paste-history dropdown — every failed retry requires re-pasting
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3170-3171 — the input is a plain <input type="text">; no autocomplete suggestions, no localStorage history, no datalist. downloadManual() never persists the query.
findings.why_it_matters: When yt-dlp fails (which happens regularly — geo-blocks, deleted videos, format errors), I have to re-find the URL in my browser history and paste again. With history I'd hit ↓ to re-pick the last query, or pick #3 from the dropdown if I want to retry the third-most-recent.
findings.recommendation: Persist last 20 successful + failed queries in localStorage. Render as a <datalist> bound to the input (zero JS, native browser dropdown), or as a styled dropdown that shows status icons (✓/✗) next to each entry. Bonus: ↑/↓ cycles through history without opening the dropdown.
findings.impact: Eliminates the 'where did I paste that URL' tax on retries. ~5-10 second savings per failed download, and most batch sessions have 2-5 failures.

findings.title: No per-card retry on a failed batch item — and the per-card failure state is lossy
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:6993-7012 runDownload() — on error it sets statusEl to '✗ failed' and re-enables the button with textContent '⬇ Album' (line 7014), losing the failure context. There is no retry button, no error tooltip, and no way to see which card failed once another card is clicked.
findings.why_it_matters: I shift-click 10 Discover albums. Three fail. I have no list of which three, no one-click retry, and the failure detail ('geo-blocked', 'video unavailable') flashes by in a toast that's already gone. To retry, I have to remember which album failed and click ⬇ Album again. This is the heart of batch workflow and it's currently a manual eyeball exercise.
findings.recommendation: Persist per-card download state (idle / downloading / done / error{message}) on the card data and re-render it. On error, replace ⬇ Album with '↻ Retry' showing the error in a tooltip. Add a batch summary at the top of the panel: '8/10 done, 2 failed — Retry failed'.
findings.impact: Turns batch downloads from manual triage into a recoverable queue. Saves several minutes per multi-track session.

findings.title: Manual panel has no bulk-paste / queue interface — only single-shot input
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3170 is a single <input type="text"> (not textarea). downloadManual() at 7018 processes exactly one query and awaits SSE completion before unlocking the button.
findings.why_it_matters: Common workflow: I have a list of 12 URLs in a notes app or chat thread. Today I have to paste, wait ~30s, paste next, wait, paste next… 12 times. A textarea that accepts newline-separated URLs and queues them through /api/download/album would let me paste once and walk away.
findings.recommendation: Add a small 'Paste list' toggle that expands the input to a textarea. Detect line breaks in the regular input on paste and offer 'Detected 12 URLs — queue all?'. Wire to existing /api/download/album SSE which already does sequential progress events.
findings.impact: Converts 12 paste-and-wait cycles into one paste. For a DJ pulling a tracklist from a chat or notes file, this is the highest-leverage single change.

findings.title: No format/quality selector — and no memory of last choice across sessions
findings.evidence: autocue/download.py:99-104 hardcodes audio_format='mp3', audio_quality='192' in the function signature. /api/download body (docs/index.html:7038) only sends {query, dest_dir} — no format pass-through. The PRD lists this as a gap.
findings.why_it_matters: 192 kbps MP3 is unacceptable for DJ playback — a 5,000-track library at this quality is audibly compressed on a club PA. I want WAV by default. Worse, even if a selector ships, if it forgets my choice every page load I'll re-set it 30 times a day. The auto-tag and loudnorm toggles have the same memory requirement.
findings.recommendation: Add format/quality dropdown next to the input (WAV / MP3 320 / Original AAC). Persist last selection in localStorage (autocue_dl_format, autocue_dl_normalize, autocue_dl_autotag). Backend: thread audio_format/audio_quality through the SSE request body.
findings.impact: Removes per-download configuration tax and the quality regret of finding 192k MP3s in the library months later.

findings.title: Destination switcher is buried and offers no quick-switch between favorite folders
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3176-3177 — destination is a <code> readout plus a single 'Switch to AutoCue folder' link (id='dl-dest-switch'). There is no folder picker UI, no list of recent destinations, no per-genre subfolder shortcut.
findings.why_it_matters: I organize by genre: ~/Music/AutoCue/house, /AutoCue/techno, /AutoCue/edits. Today the destination is a single toggle between two paths. I have to drop to a terminal or Finder to move files into the right subfolder after every download — defeating the point of a destination at all.
findings.recommendation: Replace the single switch link with a small dropdown of last 5 destinations (localStorage), a 'Pick folder…' option that uses the showDirectoryPicker File System API where available, and a 'New subfolder…' inline input that creates dest_dir + '/' + name.
findings.impact: Removes manual file-moving after batch sessions. ~10s per download saved, plus tidier library.

findings.title: Success state silently overwrites — no collision warning, no 'already downloaded' indicator
findings.evidence: autocue/download.py:149-165 — yt-dlp outtmpl is '%(title)s.%(ext)s' with no conflict handling; postprocessor will overwrite or append numbers depending on yt-dlp internals. The frontend success path (docs/index.html:7048) shows '✓ Saved to <path>' with no hint that this was a re-download or overwrote a previous file.
findings.why_it_matters: I download the same track twice by accident regularly — pull a playlist, then later pull a single I forgot was on it. Today both attempts succeed silently and I get a duplicate or an overwritten file. A 'Already in library — re-download?' check would save disk space and confusion.
findings.recommendation: Before download, check dest_dir for matching basename (or hash the YouTube ID into a sidecar manifest). On match, show 'Already downloaded as <file> on <date> — Re-download / Skip / Open file'. On success, log YouTube ID → file path in a small JSON manifest for future dedupe.
findings.impact: Prevents accidental duplicates, exposes the historical record of what's already in my library.

findings.title: No metadata-write feedback — I can't tell if ID3 tags landed
findings.evidence: autocue/download.py:156-160 — only postprocessor is FFmpegExtractAudio. No FFmpegMetadata, no EmbedThumbnail. Success event from /api/download has no fields beyond {path, status}. dl_04_success.png shows '✓ Saved to /Users/.../Aphex Twin - Avril 14th.mp3' but the file itself has no Artist/Title tags written.
findings.why_it_matters: When I drag the downloaded file into Rekordbox, it shows up with no metadata — the title is parsed from filename and Artist is blank. I have to manually tag every download, defeating the purpose of an automated pipeline. The PRD calls for an auto-tag toggle; the bigger issue is the UI must SHOW what was tagged ('Artist: Aphex Twin · Title: Avril 14th · ID3 written') so I trust it.
findings.recommendation: Add FFmpegMetadata + (optional) EmbedThumbnail postprocessors. After download, parse the resulting tags and display them in the success line: '✓ Saved: Aphex Twin — Avril 14th · 192kbps MP3 · ID3 + artwork written'. Surface in the SSE done event.
findings.impact: Cuts manual tagging time to zero. Confidence that the file is library-ready before I move it.

findings.title: Error state leaks raw stack/ffmpeg output and offers no recovery action
findings.evidence: dl_05_error.png: '✗ ERROR: ffmpeg returned exit code 1: Conversion failed!' Backend bubbles raw exception text via the SSE 'error' field (autocue/download.py raises RuntimeError; routes pass exception text through). docs/index.html:7047 shows '✗ ' + (d.error || 'failed') with no parsing.
findings.why_it_matters: 'ffmpeg returned exit code 1' is meaningless to me. I need to know: is this a geo-block? A deleted video? Out of disk? A codec issue? And what's my action — retry, try a different URL, switch format? The current error UX dead-ends.
findings.recommendation: Classify common yt-dlp/ffmpeg errors into user-facing categories: 'Video unavailable in your region — try a VPN', 'Video deleted by uploader', 'Disk full', 'Audio format not supported — try MP3 instead'. Always end with a one-click [Retry] button and [Copy error details] for support. Keep raw text behind a 'Details ▾' disclosure.
findings.impact: Turns dead-end errors into actionable recovery. Major confidence + ergonomics win.

findings.title: Confirm modal makes batch shift-click slower than single-click — and Cancel is the green primary
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3138-3153. dl_07_confirm_modal.png shows Cancel as primary green and 'Download anyway' as ghost. Every shift-click adds a confirm step.
findings.why_it_matters: I get the safety rationale (sticky Shift + accidental Enter), but for a power user who knows what they're doing, this is a per-download speed bump on the workflow that ALREADY indicated intent via Shift. And the inverted color treatment (green Cancel) is so unusual I sometimes hit Cancel thinking it's the action button. If the goal is opt-out for trained users, give me a 'Don't ask again this session' checkbox.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Don't ask again this session' checkbox to the modal. Persist in sessionStorage. Optionally swap to a one-time-per-session 'You used Shift — confirm batch downloads bypass the per-card click?' onboarding, then never re-ask. Keep Cancel as default focus but neutralize its color (ghost) — primary action should look primary.
findings.impact: Stops a per-track modal stutter once a session. Reduces miscolor confusion.

findings.title: No 'Download all suggestions' affordance on the Discover panel
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:6920-7016 — every Discover card has its own ⬇ Album button but there is no panel-level 'Download all visible suggestions' or 'Download top N' action. I count ~10-30 cards per Discover scan.
findings.why_it_matters: When Discover surfaces a great scan, I usually want everything. Today I have to shift-click each card individually, dismissing the confirm modal each time. A 'Download all (24 albums)' button that uses /api/download/album with a collective progress bar would be the workflow.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Download all (N)' or 'Download top 10' button next to the Refresh/Settings buttons in the Discover header when results are present. Hook to /api/download/album with the array of queries. Show collective 'Downloading album X of Y: <name>' progress.
findings.impact: Converts a 24-click + 24-confirm session into one click. Massive batch leverage.

findings.title: No ETA, no transfer speed, no cancel — single progress bar is information-poor
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3179-3182 — single .cue-tools-progress bar + status text. dl_03_loading.png shows '38%' with no MB/s, no ETA, no track-name confirmation that this is the right video. No cancel button is rendered.
findings.why_it_matters: On a 320kbps download from YouTube, ETA + speed tell me if the network is healthy or if yt-dlp picked a throttled endpoint. More importantly: I have NO way to cancel a running download from the manual panel. If I paste the wrong URL, I either wait or close the tab — both bad.
findings.recommendation: SSE already emits progress dicts; expose downloaded_bytes/total_bytes/speed/eta in the event. Render '12.4 MB / 38 MB · 1.8 MB/s · ETA 14s' and the resolved YouTube title under the bar. Add a [Cancel] button — backend already supports cancel_event (download.py:23-27, 137-138).
findings.impact: Builds trust mid-download and recovers from wrong-URL mistakes.

findings.title: Per-card progress bar (90px) is too narrow to read percentage and has no track-name label
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:6946 width:90px. dl_06_per_card.png: 'Downloading…' label is ellipsized at 55% with no MB/s readout. The status text fits next to a 90px bar.
findings.why_it_matters: In batch downloads I'm watching multiple cards. If one stalls at 12% I can't tell because the bar is too short to differentiate 12% from 18%. The status text 'Downloading…' is truncated and offers no info beyond the bar.
findings.recommendation: Widen the inline mini-progress to 140-160px when card width permits, OR move per-card progress into a docked queue panel (sticky at top of Discover) that shows all in-flight downloads as rows with name + bar + percent + cancel. The queue panel scales to bulk workflows much better than per-card chrome.
findings.impact: Mid — more visible per-track state during batch.

findings.title: Mobile layout pushes Download below the fold and shrinks input — paste-and-go fails on phone
findings.evidence: dl_09_mobile.png (390x844) — Download section sits below the Discover suggestions; the URL input wraps to full width but the button stays small/right-aligned. Status text '✗ ERROR: ffmpeg returned exit code 1' is wider than viewport readable.
findings.why_it_matters: I often save tracks from my phone on the train. The Download section being last-in-DOM means heavy scroll past Discover. The input field works but the success/error text overflows.
findings.recommendation: On mobile (matchMedia max-width:520px), move Download to the top of the Discover tab, OR add a floating action button that scrolls to it. Make status/error text wrap and use a smaller code block for paths. Big-thumb-target the Download button (min 44x44).
findings.impact: Makes phone workflow viable.

findings.title: Naming collision with #download-btn (Rekordbox XML) — same word, different feature, on the same screen
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3234 #download-btn 'Download XML' (Rekordbox export) is visible in the sticky bar at the same time as the Discover Download panel. Audit note also flags #backup-btn 'Download backup XML' at 2139.
findings.why_it_matters: First time I used this I clicked the wrong Download. Rekordbox XML export and YouTube audio download share a word but nothing else. A power user learns the difference; new users won't, and a DJ context-switching late at night will mis-click.
findings.recommendation: Rename #download-btn to 'Export XML' or 'Save Rekordbox XML' (it's an export, not a download). Or visually differentiate (icon + color). 'Download' should be reserved for the audio-fetch surface.
findings.impact: Low for power users (we learn it) but raises consistency / clarity score significantly.

findings.title: Disclaimer occupies more vertical real estate than the action — and shows up every session
findings.evidence: docs/index.html:3184-3186 — the legal disclaimer is ~140px of muted text below the action. Visible in every screenshot. There is no 'Acknowledged' dismiss.
findings.why_it_matters: On a daily-use surface, repeated legal text becomes blind-spot noise — and pushes useful state (progress, status) further off-screen. I read it once; show it once.
findings.recommendation: Collapse to a single-line 'Lawful use — your responsibility (?)' link that expands the full text on hover/click. After first dismiss (localStorage flag), keep it collapsed. Keep the wording itself — just compress the visual weight.
findings.impact: Reclaims ~100px of vertical space on every Discover tab visit.
